input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Digital Twin system under study** focuses on a **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl** industrial robot controller integrated with a **MABI MAX 100** machining system, primarily used for **precision manufacturing tasks** in an **Industry 4.0** context. The physical system comprises:
  - **Controller**: Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl (a CNC controller for motion control and automation),
  - **Robot Arm**: MABI MAX 100 (a collaborative or industrial robot designed for machining applications),
  - **Operational Domain**: **Continuous process optimization** and **real-time motion accuracy monitoring** in manufacturing environments,
  - **Key Characteristics**:
    - **Real-time data acquisition** from the physical system via **automated one-way data flow** (as per the Digital Shadow Reference Model),
    - **High-precision motion control** requirements for machining tasks,
    - **Integration with simulation and modeling tools** for behavior analysis and performance validation,
    - **Passive data aggregation** (no bidirectional control loop; data is collected for contextual analysis rather than direct system intervention).
  The system is explicitly studied for **decision support in optimization** (e.g., process adjustments, predictive maintenance) rather than autonomous control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin system** provides the following **targeted services** to users (e.g., engineers, operators, and decision-makers) and the physical system:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - **Real-time dashboard** for visualizing system states, performance metrics (e.g., motion accuracy, tool wear), and operational data traces.
    - **Aggregation and abstraction** of contextual data (e.g., sensor readings, controller logs) for **purpose-specific analysis** (e.g., process optimization).
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Predictive analytics** for identifying deviations in motion accuracy or tool performance, enabling **proactive adjustments** in machining parameters.
    - **Simulation-driven optimization** using **SysML-based models** to validate changes before physical implementation.
  - **Visualization**:
    - **SysML-based diagrams** (e.g., Internal Block Diagrams) for representing functional and logical architectures of the robot and controller.
    - **Data-driven dashboards** (not interactive 3D/4D models) to present aggregated metrics and trends.
  - **Excluded Services (per Document Constraints)**:
    - **No bidirectional control** (contradicts the **one-way data flow** definition of Digital Shadows; no feedback mechanisms to adjust the physical system).
    - **No autonomous operation** (the system is passive; active components for interpretation are external to the Digital Twin itself).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The documents highlight the following tools, technologies, and enablers for implementing Digital Twins:

    - **Software Platforms and Frameworks**:
      - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) Tools**:
        - **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language) for functional/logical architecture modeling.
        - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: ACME, AADL, MontiArc (for component-connector perspectives).
      - **Simulation Engines**:
        - **MathWorks Simulink** for detailed behavior modeling.
        - Other simulation tools/methods (e.g., [29], [30]) for hybrid simulations.
      - **General-Purpose Languages**:
        - **C++**, **Java** for implementing system behavior or control logic.

    - **Development Tools**:
      - **Modeling Notations**: RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT for layered system abstraction.
      - **State Machines**: SysML v1.7 state machines or MontiArc Automaton for behavior description.
      - **Code Generation**: Tools to translate models into executable code (e.g., for embedded systems).

    - **Databases and Storage**:
      - **Contextual Data Aggregation**: Storage systems for sensor data, logs, and operational traces (not explicitly named in docs but implied for digital shadows/twins).
      - **Integration with IoT/Industry 4.0 Platforms**: For real-time data ingestion (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT).

    - **Specialized Technologies**:
      - **Digital Shadow Reference Model**: UML class diagrams for defining digital shadow concepts (e.g., one-way data flow).
      - **AI/ML Integration**: Implied for predictive capabilities (e.g., [62] mentions AI-driven DTs for smart manufacturing).
      - **Co-Simulation**: Combining physical and digital models for validation (e.g., [28], [29]).
      - **Visualization Tools**: Interactive 3D/4D models for stakeholders (e.g., SysML-based visualizations).

    - **Key Enablers**:
      - **Heterogeneous Stakeholder Integration**: Tools to unify diverse perspectives (e.g., [31]).
      - **Bidirectional Communication**: Active components in DTs to process data and feed insights back to physical systems.
      - **Modularity**: Support for multi-tool workflows (e.g., SysML + Simulink + custom code).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system described in the documents emphasizes **temporal synchronization** through structured data trace aggregation and abstraction, where **data traces** (collected from physical systems) are processed with **latency-sensitive computational steps** within the Processing component. The reference model supports **fine-grained traceability** of data traces back to their originating processing components, ensuring temporal alignment between physical and digital states. While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms) or latency requirements are not quantified, the system relies on **real-time or near-real-time data flows** (implied by the one-way data aggregation in digital shadows) and **transitive traceability** to maintain temporal consistency. The use of **simulation and data calculation models** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools) further enables **time-scale abstraction** for higher-level computations, though granularity specifics (e.g., microsecond vs. second-level updates) are not detailed in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system adheres to a **single-instance, purpose-driven design**, where each Digital Twin instance is created for a **specific asset** and a **particular purpose**, as outlined in the concretization of the Digital Shadow Reference Model. There is no explicit support for **multiple Digital Twin instances** or **decentralized architectures**; instead, the system follows a **centralized processing architecture** where data traces are aggregated, processed, and abstracted in a unified Processing Component. Hierarchical structures or **multi-level Digital Twin constellations** (e.g., parent-child relationships) are not mentioned. The scope of responsibility is confined to the **concrete realization** of the reference model, ensuring alignment with the asset’s operational requirements without redundancy or distribution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system’s **orchestration and coordination** are structured around a **reference model-based approach**, where **conforming models** (e.g., Class Diagrams) incarnate abstract concepts into concrete elements. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** defines a **processing-centric architecture** with:
   - **Processing components** (encoding logic via data calculation or simulation models),
   - **Data traces** (aggregated from physical systems),
   - **Metadata** (supporting further processing),
   - **Traceability links** (associations between traces, components, and models).
   Orchestration relies on **methodological concretization** of reference models (e.g., mapping reference concepts to use-case-specific elements) and **fine-grained traceability** to ensure system-wide consistency. The system does not explicitly mention **architecture patterns** (e.g., microservices, event-driven) or **resource management strategies**, but the **modular, purpose-driven design** implies **adaptive coordination** via conformance relations and hierarchical traceability. Integration of components is implied through **heterogeneous modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, general-purpose languages) and **data-driven workflows**.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with external systems primarily through **MQTT for live data streaming** and **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** for standardized asset representation, as explicitly mentioned in the documents. No other industrial data exchange protocols (e.g., OPC UA, REST, SOAP, ESBs) are referenced. The integration follows a **purpose-driven data flow**, where external data (e.g., from IoT sensors, enterprise systems) is ingested as **data traces** and processed by the Digital Twin’s Processing Components. The absence of explicit mention of RESTful APIs, SOAP services, or enterprise service buses (ESBs) indicates that integration is **protocol-specific to MQTT and AAS**, with no broader interoperability frameworks described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system integrates **heterogeneous computational models and data components** to represent system structure and behavior. **Modeling frameworks** include **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies such as **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for functional and logical architecture, including **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector perspectives. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like **AADL (Architecture Analysis & Design Language)** and **MontiArc** are employed to describe components, interfaces, and interconnections, with **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines** capturing behavioral dynamics. For detailed behavioral modeling, **MathWorks Simulink** and other simulation tools are integrated, alongside **general-purpose languages (C++, Java)** for low-level implementations.

The system combines **white-box (physics-based) models** (e.g., **Simulink-based dynamic simulations**) with **black-box (data-driven) models** (e.g., **machine learning or probabilistic latent variable models**—*not explicitly mentioned in the provided documents, but implied in the broader context of friction modeling*). Data sources include **real-time sensor streams** (e.g., position encoders, current measurements) and **historical operational data**, managed via **MQTT-based streaming protocols**. Model relationships are established through **traceability mechanisms** (e.g., transformations from SysML to simulation models) to ensure consistency across **requirements, functional, logical, and physical layers** (RFLP methodology). Data aggregation and abstraction are purpose-driven, aligning with **digital shadow frameworks** as described in Kritzinger et al. [32], where contextual data traces are collected for specific applications (e.g., industrial robot friction analysis).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system incorporates a **sensing infrastructure** comprising **two position encoders per drive**: one mounted on the **permanent magnet synchronous motor** and a secondary encoder positioned behind the **gear transmission** (including a **cycloidal gear**). These encoders measure **input current** and **output angle** with a **repeatability of 0.06 mm**. Data is **streamed via MQTT** for real-time transmission to the digital representation. The system does not explicitly mention **CAN, EtherCAT, or sub-micrometer resolution** in the provided documents; the specified repeatability is **0.06 mm** (not sub-micrometer).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system prioritizes **fidelity** through the integration of **white-box (physics-based) and black-box (data-driven) models**, ensuring comprehensive representation of nonlinear phenomena (e.g., robot friction). **Validation** is achieved via **deviation analysis between simulated and measured data**, as referenced in Section IV.A. **Traceability** is emphasized in model-based development to verify correctness of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models). However, explicit details on **uncertainty quantification, error detection mechanisms, or quality assurance processes** are not provided in the documents. The system relies on **probabilistic latent variable models** (implied in broader research but not explicitly cited here) and **adaptive control methods** for reliability, though these are not detailed in the given context.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the described Digital Twin (DT) system involves a **unidirectional, automated data flow** from physical objects to their digital representations, characterized as a **digital shadow** per Kritzinger et al. [32]. This flow includes:
- **Data Types Transmitted**: Contextual data traces (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs, environmental parameters) aggregated and abstracted for specific manufacturing purposes [32].
- **Communication Protocols**: While not explicitly named in the documents, the context implies **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** as a plausible protocol (referenced in [52] for IoT data transmission) and **ThingsBoard** (an open-source IoT platform [53]) for data aggregation, suggesting lightweight, publish-subscribe mechanisms.
- **Transmission Frequencies**: Not specified in the documents, but manufacturing digital shadows (e.g., CNC machines [57]) typically operate in **real-time or near-real-time intervals** (e.g., milliseconds to seconds), depending on sensor granularity.
- **Event Triggers**: Data collection is triggered by **physical events** (e.g., machine state changes, operational anomalies) or **scheduled intervals** for periodic updates [56].
- **Data Quality Assurance**: Implicitly addressed via **aggregation and abstraction** processes [32], though no explicit methods (e.g., filtering, normalization, or validation rules) are detailed in the provided documents.
- **Preprocessing Steps**: Includes **contextual aggregation** (e.g., grouping sensor data) and **abstraction** to derive higher-level insights (e.g., machine health indicators) [32, 57]. Tools like **SysML** or **Simulink** [28] may be used for post-processing in MBSE workflows [1].
- **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Heterogeneous data sources are integrated via **model-driven software engineering (MDSE) tools** (e.g., [58]) or **open-source platforms** like ThingsBoard [53], enabling interoperability across tools and stakeholders [31]. No specific integration frameworks (e.g., API gateways, ETL pipelines) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents for Digital Twins. However, the distinction between **digital shadows** (passive, one-way data flow) and **Digital Twins** (active, bidirectional) [32] implies that DTs may include:
- **Control Flows**: Hypothetical **command types** (e.g., parameter adjustments, maintenance triggers) sent from the digital model to physical systems, though no specific formats or protocols are described.
- **Validation/Authentication**: Not mentioned; security mechanisms (e.g., OAuth, TLS) are implied but unspecified.
- **Feedback Systems**: No explicit feedback loops (e.g., closed-loop control) are referenced in the documents.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Not addressed; potential conflicts between digital and physical states are not discussed.
- **Command Execution Confirmation**: Not detailed; no mention of acknowledgment mechanisms or retry policies.
**Conclusion**: This characteristic is **Not in Document** for the described system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for this system is primarily defined by the **MQTT protocol**, which facilitates the **one-way streaming of sensor data** from physical objects to their digital shadow. No additional details regarding network topology (e.g., hybrid, star, mesh), bandwidth requirements, latency specifications, or redundancy mechanisms are provided. Security protocols or encryption methods (e.g., TLS) are not mentioned. The system’s communication is unidirectional, aligning with the **digital shadow reference model** as a passive data receiver.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **SysML** for architecture modeling and **MathWorks Simulink** for executable simulation models to represent system behavior. The deployment infrastructure is not explicitly detailed, but the use of **MBSE tools** implies a **model-driven approach** for development and execution. No specific hosting platforms (cloud, edge, or on-premise) are mentioned, nor are computational/storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, or deployment models (e.g., containerized, virtualized) addressed. The focus remains on **modeling and simulation tools** rather than deployment specifics.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system, as implied by the provided documents, is structured through a **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** framework, which includes distinct abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. These layers are managed via methodologies such as **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**, which guide the transition between conceptual and implementation phases. The lifecycle governance involves **conformance relations** between reference models (e.g., Digital Shadow Reference Model) and concrete realizations, ensuring traceability via **UML Class Diagrams (CDs)** and **association mappings** that link data traces to processing components. The lifecycle also incorporates **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** to align modeling notations (e.g., SysML, AADL, MontiArc) and tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose languages like C++/Java) across phases. Transition management is implicit in the **purpose-driven concretization** of reference models, where designers adapt abstract concepts (e.g., data traces, processing logic) to domain-specific use cases (e.g., injection molding) while maintaining **fine-grained traceability** through transitive associations of data traces to models and components.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development of Digital Twins (DTs) follows a **purpose-driven methodology** rooted in **Digital Shadow (DS) creation**, as outlined in the documents. The process begins with **data-centric or requirement-driven design**, where relevant data traces are collected from cyber-physical systems (CPS) or derived from specific objectives (e.g., predictive maintenance, process optimization). Key methodologies include **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** with **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **SysML IBDs**, **AADL**, or **MontiArc**, which define components, interfaces, and connectors. The evolution of DTs involves **concretization of reference models** (e.g., Digital Shadow Reference Model) into **conforming models** via **Class Diagram (CD) incarnations**, ensuring alignment with domain-specific requirements. Quality assurance is implied through **traceability mechanisms** (e.g., transitive associations of data traces to processing components and models) and **fine-grained traceability** enabled by **processing components** (e.g., data calculation or simulation models). Version control and evolution management are not explicitly detailed, but the framework supports **iterative refinement** of models (e.g., combining SysML with Simulink or C++/Java) to adapt to changing system dynamics or stakeholder needs.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system generates **decision-support capabilities** through **Processing Components** that consume input data traces (e.g., sensor data, operational metrics) and produce output data via **data calculation or simulation models**. These models enable **higher-level abstractions** (e.g., predictive analytics, performance simulations) to support insights. The system leverages **traceability** (via associations in UML CDs) to link data traces to their origins (e.g., processing components, models) and dependencies, ensuring **confidence measures** for recommendations. User interaction is implied through **heterogeneous stakeholder integration**, where insights are derived for diverse roles (e.g., engineers, operators) via **modeling tools** (e.g., Simulink, SysML). However, specific **analytics methods** (e.g., machine learning, statistical modeling) or **decision-making processes** (e.g., real-time alerts, optimization algorithms) are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system for the MABI robot incorporates a **safety cell** restriction to ensure operational safety, limiting the maximum working range to **[-90°, 90°]**, as explicitly documented in the context of friction modeling and robot dynamics (Trinh et al., 2023). While the system streams data via **MQTT** (Light, 2017), no explicit security protocols (e.g., TLS, OAuth, or role-based access control) are described. The Digital Shadow framework (Heithoff et al., 2025) emphasizes **motion accuracy** and **understanding of physical behavior** but does not detail fail-safe mechanisms, threat mitigation, or risk assessment methodologies. The provided documents do not reference **RAMI 4.0** or **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** security standards, nor do they mention authentication systems, consent management, or data anonymization techniques. The only safety-related specification is the **geometric confinement** of the robot's operational envelope, which serves as a basic safety measure.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

It correctly cites the repeatability (0.06 mm) and data transmission method (MQTT) from the text. It also accurately notes the absence of other protocols like CAN or EtherCAT. Quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox', 'high path repeatability (0.06 mm)', 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'.

The extraction accurately describes the validation approach using deviation analysis between simulated and measured data, which is explicitly stated in Section IV.A. It correctly identifies the use of white-box and black-box models to improve fidelity and the emphasis on traceability from Section II.B. It also correctly notes that specific uncertainty quantification details are not provided. Quotes: 'solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'traceability of how data within a DS has been generated', 'resulting digital shadows enable the continuous... identification of joint friction, using both white-box and black-box models'.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
